Challenges include: the inherent rarity of training data (some syndromes have fewer than 100 known cases), ethical concerns about facial analysis technology (especially in non-European populations where training data is sparse), and the emotional impact of AI-delivered rare disease diagnoses on families.

documents.title: Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Treatment Regimes in Chronic Disease
documents.text: Chronic diseases require ongoing treatment adjustments based on patient response — a sequential decision-making problem well-suited to reinforcement learning (RL). This paper reviews RL applications in dynamic treatment regimes for chronic conditions. In type 2 diabetes, offline RL algorithms trained on EHR data from 120,000 patients optimized insulin dosing and oral medication sequencing. The RL policy recommended lower doses of insulin with more aggressive use of SGLT2 inhibitors compared to standard clinical practice, resulting in estimated HbA1c reductions of 0.4% with 30% fewer hypoglycemic episodes. In sepsis management, the AI Clinician (from Imperial College London) used a fitted Q-iteration approach on MIMIC-IV data to learn optimal vasopressor and IV fluid strategies. Retrospective analysis showed that patients whose actual treatment aligned with the AI policy had 3.6% lower mortality than those whose treatment diverged. For cancer treatment, RL has been applied to radiation therapy dose fractionation — adapting dose schedules based on tumor response during treatment. An RL policy trained on 8,000 glioblastoma cases reduced normal tissue toxicity by 15% while maintaining tumor control probability. Key methodological challenges include: (1) off-policy evaluation — assessing RL policies without deploying them on real patients requires sophisticated counterfactual estimation, (2) safety constraints — RL must explore the action space without recommending dangerous treatments, addressed through constrained MDPs and conservative Q-learning, (3) sample efficiency — clinical data is expensive and sparse compared to simulation environments, and (4) interpretability — clinicians need to understand why the RL agent recommends a particular action, leading to interest in attention-based RL architectures that highlight which patient features drive recommendations.

documents.title: AI-Powered Lab Diagnostics: Automating Blood Work and Beyond
documents.text: Clinical laboratories process billions of tests annually, and AI is transforming every stage of the laboratory workflow. Pre-analytical AI: Beckman Coulter's DxA 5000 uses computer vision to inspect sample quality (hemolysis, lipemia, icterus) before analysis, reducing pre-analytical errors by 62%. Analytical AI: Sysmex's XR-Series hematology analyzers integrate AI to classify white blood cells from scatter plots, reducing manual differential counts by 80%. SigTuple (India) has developed AI microscopy that performs complete blood counts, peripheral smear analysis, and urine sediment analysis at 95-97% concordance with manual review. Post-analytical AI: AI systems flag critical value patterns — for example, detecting early disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) by recognizing the constellation of falling platelets, rising D-dimer, and prolonging PT/aPTT across sequential lab results, alerting clinicians before any single value reaches a critical threshold. Point-of-care: Companies like Sight Diagnostics (OLO, FDA-cleared complete blood count from a finger prick in minutes using AI image analysis) and Genalyte (now part of Roper Technologies, multiplexed immunoassay with AI interpretation) are bringing lab-quality AI diagnostics to the bedside. The global clinical laboratory AI market is estimated at $2.4 billion in 2025, projected to reach $8.7 billion by 2030. Key trends include: total lab automation (TLA) lines incorporating AI quality control, mass spectrometry data interpretation with ML for microorganism identification (MALDI-TOF), and the integration of lab AI with EHR systems for real-time clinical decision support.

documents.title: Ethical AI in Healthcare: Frameworks for Responsible Development and Deployment
documents.text: As healthcare AI proliferates, the need for actionable ethical frameworks has become urgent. This paper synthesizes 23 existing frameworks (including those from WHO, OECD, IEEE, AMA, and individual health systems) and proposes a unified 7-principle framework for responsible healthcare AI: (1) Clinical beneficence — AI must demonstrate measurable patient benefit, not merely technical performance. (2) Non-maleficence — proactive harm assessment including bias audits, failure mode analysis, and adversarial testing. (3) Autonomy preservation — patients must be informed about AI involvement in their care and retain the right to opt out. (4) Justice and equity — performance must be validated across demographic subgroups with mandatory disaggregated reporting. (5) Transparency — both the model's reasoning (explainability) and the organization's deployment practices (disclosure) must be transparent. (6) Accountability — clear chains of responsibility from developer to deploying clinician, with liability frameworks for AI-related adverse events. (7) Privacy and data stewardship — compliance with data protection regulations and ethical data use beyond legal minimums. The paper also proposes a maturity model for healthcare organizations deploying AI: Level 1 (Ad hoc — individual departments experimenting), Level 2 (Managed — centralized AI governance committee), Level 3 (Defined — standardized validation, procurement, and monitoring processes), Level 4 (Optimized — continuous learning systems with real-time bias monitoring and automated performance alerts). A survey of 200 US health systems found that 72% are at Level 1, 18% at Level 2, 8% at Level 3, and fewer than 2% at Level 4.

documents.title: The Economics of Healthcare AI: ROI Analysis Across Use Cases
documents.text: Proving return on investment remains the critical challenge for healthcare AI adoption. This report analyzes ROI data from 150 healthcare AI deployments across 48 health systems. We categorize use cases by ROI timeline: Quick wins (positive ROI within 6 months): Ambient AI scribes (ROI 3:1 from reduced documentation time and physician retention), AI coding/billing (ROI 5:1 from reduced denials and faster A/R), and radiology AI triage (ROI 2:1 from workflow efficiency). These account for 60% of current deployments. Medium-term value (6-18 months): Predictive staffing (ROI 2.5:1 from reduced overtime and agency costs), clinical trial matching (ROI 4:1 from accelerated enrollment for pharma partners), and patient flow optimization (ROI 2:1 from reduced ED boarding and increased throughput). Strategic investments (18+ months): Diagnostic AI (ROI difficult to quantify directly — value measured in improved outcomes, reduced liability), drug discovery AI (ROI potentially massive but realized over 5-10 year development cycles), and population health AI (ROI depends on value-based care contract structures). The median total cost of ownership for an enterprise healthcare AI deployment is $1.8 million in year one (licensing, integration, training, monitoring) and $600K annually thereafter. The biggest hidden cost is integration — connecting AI tools to existing EHR workflows, PACS systems, and laboratory information systems accounts for 40-60% of first-year costs. Health systems report that AI projects fail most often due to: poor change management (31%), data quality issues (27%), misaligned incentives (22%), and technical integration challenges (20%). The report recommends starting with high-ROI, low-integration-complexity use cases (scribes, coding) to build institutional AI competency before tackling complex clinical AI.

documents.title: Ophthalmology AI: Beyond Diabetic Retinopathy to Multimodal Eye Disease Screening
documents.text: While diabetic retinopathy screening has been the showcase application for ophthalmic AI, the field has expanded rapidly to encompass multiple eye diseases. For glaucoma, AI models analyzing optical coherence tomography (OCT) and visual fields can detect glaucomatous progression 18 months earlier than clinical assessment (AUROC 0.94 for progression detection). For age-related macular degeneration (AMD), DeepMind/Moorfields' model predicts conversion from dry to wet AMD 6 months in advance with 80% sensitivity, enabling earlier anti-VEGF treatment. For myopia progression in children — a growing global epidemic affecting 50% of young adults in East Asia — AI systems trained on axial length measurements and corneal topography predict high myopia risk (>-6D) with 85% accuracy, guiding prophylactic atropine treatment timing. The most significant development is multimodal retinal imaging AI that screens for systemic diseases: AI analysis of retinal fundus photographs can detect cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, smoking status, HbA1c level) with moderate accuracy (AUC 0.70-0.85), predict 5-year cardiovascular event risk, and detect early signs of neurodegeneration (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's) from retinal nerve fiber layer thinning. This positions the retinal photograph as a potential universal screening tool — a 'window to systemic health.' Companies leading this space include Google Health, TOPCON Healthcare, and EyeNuk (FDA-cleared autonomous DR screening). The key challenge is integrating retinal AI screening into primary care workflows where eye imaging equipment is not standard.

documents.title: AI in Dentistry: Automated X-Ray Interpretation and Treatment Planning
documents.text: Dentistry is an emerging frontier for healthcare AI, driven by the visual nature of dental diagnostics and the high volume of dental radiographs (over 500 million taken annually in the US alone). Leading dental AI companies include: Overjet (FDA-cleared for dental pathology detection on periapical and bitewing X-rays, deployed in 80+ dental service organizations), Pearl (FDA-cleared for caries, periapical pathology, and calculus detection, integrated with major practice management systems), and Dentistry.AI (focused on orthodontic treatment planning). Overjet's AI detects 10 dental conditions including caries, bone loss, and calculus with sensitivity ranging from 85-95% depending on the condition. In a study of 4,000 radiographs, AI-assisted dentists detected 37% more early caries lesions compared to unassisted reading. For insurance, dental AI is being used on both sides: Delta Dental uses Overjet to automatically assess the clinical necessity of submitted claims (reducing improper payments by 28%), while dental practices use the same AI to ensure their documentation supports claim approval. Treatment planning AI is also advancing: Align Technology (Invisalign) uses AI to predict tooth movement and design optimal aligner sequences, reducing treatment time by 20%. Implant planning AI (from companies like Diagnocat and BlueSkyBio) creates surgical guides from CBCT scans with sub-millimeter accuracy. Challenges include: the fragmented nature of dental practices (85% are solo or small group practices with limited IT infrastructure), lower reimbursement rates limiting technology investment, and the need for regulatory clarity — the FDA has cleared dental AI for 'detection assistance' but the line between assistance and autonomous diagnosis remains blurry.

documents.title: Generative AI for Medical Education and Training
documents.text: Generative AI is transforming medical education across the learning continuum. For preclinical education, tools like OpenEvidence and Glass Health generate clinical reasoning exercises from real patient case data, creating an unlimited supply of practice cases calibrated to student level. Medical schools including Harvard, Stanford, and Johns Hopkins are piloting LLM-based tutoring systems that provide personalized Socratic feedback on clinical reasoning — early data suggests students using AI tutors show 15% improvement in clinical reasoning assessments. For clinical training, generative AI creates realistic simulated patient conversations. Anthropic's Claude and OpenAI's GPT-4 are being used to power standardized patient simulations that can portray complex emotional scenarios (breaking bad news, addressing vaccine hesitancy) with more consistency than human actors. The University of California San Francisco reported that AI-simulated OSCE stations correlated 0.82 with human-actor stations in assessing communication skills. For continuing medical education (CME), AI personalizes learning to knowledge gaps — analyzing a physician's prescribing patterns and patient outcomes to recommend targeted education. The most ambitious application is AI-generated surgical simulation: using 3D models derived from patient imaging data, combined with physics simulation and generative AI for tissue behavior, surgical residents can rehearse complex procedures on patient-specific virtual anatomy. Key concerns include: over-reliance on AI for knowledge (reducing deep learning), the potential for AI-generated clinical scenarios to contain errors that teach incorrect information, and ensuring equitable access to AI educational tools across resource-constrained medical schools globally.

documents.title: Antibiotic Resistance: AI Discovers New Classes of Antibiotics
documents.text: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) kills an estimated 1.27 million people annually and is projected to cause 10 million deaths per year by 2050. The antibiotic pipeline has been stagnant — no new class of antibiotics was discovered between 1987 and 2023. AI is breaking this drought. In 2024, researchers at MIT used a graph neural network trained on 39,000 compounds to identify halicin, a structurally novel antibiotic effective against pan-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii. Building on this, the same group expanded their search using a molecular transformer model trained on 12 million compounds, discovering abaucin — the first species-specific antibiotic targeting A. baumannii while sparing beneficial gut bacteria. In 2025, a separate effort by Phare Bio (spun out of McMaster University) used diffusion models to generate entirely novel molecular structures with predicted antibiotic activity, synthesizing and validating 8 compounds with activity against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Three have entered preclinical development. The AI approach offers a key advantage: it can be directed to find antibiotics with specific properties (narrow-spectrum, resistant to known resistance mechanisms, oral bioavailability) by encoding these as optimization objectives. The challenge is the 'valley of death' between AI discovery and clinical availability — even with AI acceleration, antibiotic development takes 5-10 years and costs $300M-$1B, and the economics of antibiotics (short courses, low pricing, stewardship pressure) make them unattractive to pharma. Policy proposals include subscription-based 'Netflix models' for antibiotic reimbursement (already piloted in the UK) and extended exclusivity periods for novel antibiotics to improve economic viability.

documents.title: AI for Hospital-Acquired Infection Prevention
documents.text: Hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) affect 1 in 31 hospital patients in the US, causing approximately 99,000 deaths and $33 billion in excess costs annually. AI is being applied to HAI prevention through three main approaches: (1) Predictive risk models: ML models that identify patients at highest risk of developing specific HAIs (catheter-associated UTI, central line-associated bloodstream infection, surgical site infection, C. difficile) from admission data and ongoing clinical trajectory. A multicenter study across 15 hospitals showed that a gradient-boosted model predicted C. difficile infection 48 hours before clinical diagnosis with AUROC 0.87, enabling proactive isolation and antibiotic stewardship interventions. (2) Surveillance automation: NLP systems that automatically scan clinical notes, microbiology results, and nursing assessments to identify HAI events that meet CDC/NHSN definitions, replacing the labor-intensive manual surveillance process. Automated surveillance achieved 94% sensitivity and 97% specificity compared to manual review, saving infection preventionists an estimated 15 hours per week. (3) Environmental monitoring: Computer vision systems in ICUs that monitor hand hygiene compliance (detecting whether healthcare workers use hand sanitizer or wash hands at room entry/exit). AI-monitored hand hygiene compliance averaged 82%, compared to 48% measured by human observers (the Hawthorne effect explains most of this gap). Hospitals deploying AI hand hygiene monitoring saw a 14% reduction in HAI rates over 12 months. The main challenge is integrating these disparate AI systems into a unified infection prevention dashboard that provides actionable intelligence to infection control teams in real-time.

documents.title: Climate Change and AI in Healthcare: Modeling Health Impacts and Adaptation
documents.text: Climate change is increasingly recognized as a health emergency, and AI is being deployed to model, predict, and mitigate its health impacts. Heat-related mortality is projected to increase by 370% by 2050 under current emissions trajectories. AI models combining weather forecasts, urban heat island maps, demographic vulnerability data, and health system capacity can predict heat-related ED surges 3-5 days in advance with 78% accuracy, enabling preemptive resource allocation. For vector-borne diseases, ML models trained on climate, land use, and entomological data predict geographic spread of dengue, malaria, and Lyme disease with 2-4 week lead times. These models have been deployed in 12 countries through the WHO's climate-health surveillance network. For air quality, Google's AI-powered fire tracking system (combined with atmospheric modeling) predicts PM2.5 exposure at zip-code resolution, enabling targeted health advisories for asthma and COPD patients via EHR-integrated alerts. AI is also optimizing healthcare's own carbon footprint — the health sector contributes 4.4% of global greenhouse gas emissions. AI-optimized operating room scheduling (reducing idle HVAC and lighting), supply chain logistics (reducing medical supply transport emissions), and predictive maintenance for hospital energy systems can reduce health system emissions by an estimated 20-30%. Key challenges include: the computational carbon footprint of AI itself (training a large model can emit as much CO2 as 5 cars over their lifetimes), the digital divide (climate-vulnerable populations often lack digital infrastructure for AI-enabled interventions), and the fundamental tension between AI's resource demands and sustainability goals.

documents.title: Pediatric AI: Unique Challenges in Developing AI for Children's Health
documents.text: Developing AI for pediatric populations presents unique challenges that are often overlooked in the adult-centric healthcare AI landscape. Children are not small adults — their physiology changes rapidly, normal vital sign ranges vary by age, diseases present differently, and ethical consent frameworks are more complex. This review identifies five key challenges: (1) Data scarcity: Children represent 22% of the US population but account for only 8% of EHR data used in AI training. Pediatric datasets are smaller and more fragmented across children's hospitals. (2) Age-dependent baselines: A heart rate of 150 bpm is concerning in an adult but normal in an infant. AI models must incorporate age-adjusted reference ranges, requiring continuous calibration rather than fixed thresholds. (3) Growth and development: AI models must account for growth trajectories — a BMI percentile, bone age, or developmental milestone that is normal at one age is abnormal at another. Bone age assessment AI (by BoneXpert, deployed in 1,000+ hospitals) has shown that AI matches radiologist accuracy but improves consistency (reducing inter-reader variability from 11 months to 3 months). (4) Rare diseases: Pediatric-onset genetic diseases are individually rare but collectively common, affecting 1 in 12 children. AI tools like Face2Gene and PhenoTips (discussed in DOC-044) are particularly valuable in pediatrics. (5) Ethical considerations: Children cannot consent to AI-informed care, raising questions about proxy consent, long-term data use (a child's health data may persist for 80+ years), and the right to an 'open future' unaffected by predictive health labels. The review calls for dedicated pediatric AI benchmarks, mandatory age-stratified validation for any AI tool used in mixed-age populations, and increased federal funding for pediatric health data infrastructure.

documents.title: Natural Language Processing for Social Media Disease Surveillance
documents.text: Social media platforms generate vast amounts of health-relevant data, from self-reported symptoms to medication experiences. NLP-powered infodemiology — the study of health information patterns in digital data — has matured into a practical public health tool. Current applications include: (1) Syndromic surveillance: Models analyzing Twitter/X, Reddit, and Weibo posts detect disease outbreaks 1-2 weeks before traditional surveillance systems. During the 2024-2025 flu season, an NLP system tracking flu-related tweets predicted state-level ILI (influenza-like illness) rates with 0.92 correlation to CDC data, providing earlier signals than sentinel physician networks. (2) Drug safety: FDA and pharma companies use NLP to mine social media for adverse drug reaction (ADR) reports. A study analyzing 2.3 million medication-related Reddit posts identified 1,247 potential ADR signals, 23% of which were not in existing drug labels. (3) Mental health monitoring: NLP models analyzing language patterns on social media can identify individuals at risk of depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation. The CLEF eRisk shared task has benchmarked these models since 2017, with current best systems achieving F1 scores of 0.65-0.72 for depression detection from Reddit posts. (4) Vaccine sentiment: During COVID-19 vaccination campaigns, NLP tracked vaccine hesitancy trends across platforms, identifying specific misinformation narratives in real-time. Ethical challenges are profound: using social media data for health surveillance raises consent issues (users don't post expecting their data to be analyzed for health signals), privacy risks (de-anonymization), potential for discrimination (employers or insurers using social media health signals), and the inherent biases of social media populations (younger, more urban, more tech-savvy than the general population).

documents.title: Conversational AI for Chronic Disease Management: Diabetes and Heart Failure
documents.text: Chronic diseases account for 75% of US healthcare spending and require ongoing patient self-management — a domain where conversational AI is showing measurable impact. This review examines AI chatbot interventions for diabetes and heart failure management. For type 2 diabetes, the most-studied systems are: Lark Health (AI health coach, 500,000+ users, demonstrated 1.0% HbA1c reduction in a 12-month RCT), Livongo (acquired by Teladoc, combines connected glucometer with AI coaching, 0.8% HbA1c reduction), and DarioHealth (AI + human coaching hybrid, 0.6% HbA1c reduction). These platforms provide personalized meal recommendations, medication reminders, blood glucose trend analysis, and motivational support via conversational interfaces. For heart failure, the most impactful intervention is AI-analyzed remote weight monitoring — daily weight gain is an early sign of fluid retention and decompensation. AI systems that integrate weight trends, blood pressure, heart rate, and symptom diaries to alert care teams have reduced 30-day heart failure readmissions by 22-38% across multiple studies. The conversational component matters: patients who engage with an AI chatbot (vs. passive monitoring alone) show 40% higher adherence to monitoring protocols and 25% better medication compliance. The key insight from this review is that conversational AI's value lies not in replacing clinical judgment but in maintaining continuous engagement between clinic visits — the average diabetes patient sees their endocrinologist for 20 minutes twice a year, leaving 525,580 minutes of self-management per year where AI can provide support.

documents.title: Explainable AI in Healthcare: Methods, Challenges, and Clinical Acceptance
documents.text: The 'black box' nature of deep learning models remains a significant barrier to clinical adoption of healthcare AI. This review evaluates explainable AI (XAI) methods in healthcare across 89 studies. The most commonly used XAI methods in healthcare are: (1) SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) — used in 42% of studies, provides feature-level importance scores. In clinical risk prediction, SHAP explanations were rated as 'helpful' by 78% of surveyed clinicians. (2) Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) — used in 38% of medical imaging studies, highlights image regions driving predictions. For lung nodule detection, Grad-CAM overlays helped radiologists verify AI predictions, increasing trust from 54% to 82%. (3) Attention visualization — particularly relevant for NLP models processing clinical text, showing which parts of a clinical note influenced the prediction. (4) Concept-based explanations — an emerging approach that explains predictions in terms of clinical concepts rather than raw features (e.g., 'this prediction is driven by the combination of elevated troponin, ST-segment changes, and chest pain' rather than 'features 47, 102, and 215 have high importance'). A critical finding: the form of explanation matters more than its existence. Clinicians prefer causal explanations ('the model predicts sepsis because lactate is rising and heart rate variability is declining') over correlational ones ('features X, Y, Z are important'). However, current XAI methods are predominantly correlational. The review also found that excessive explanation detail can paradoxically reduce trust — clinicians exposed to overly complex SHAP waterfall plots were less likely to follow AI recommendations than those given simple 'top 3 reasons' summaries. The recommended approach is layered explanations: a simple summary for routine use with drill-down detail available on demand.

documents.title: AI and the Nursing Profession: Documentation, Monitoring, and Workforce Impact
documents.text: Nurses, who represent the largest healthcare workforce segment (4.7 million RNs in the US), are both key beneficiaries and stakeholders in healthcare AI deployment. Current AI applications impacting nursing include: (1) Documentation: Ambient AI scribes (Abridge, DAX Copilot) are beginning to extend beyond physician notes to generate nursing assessments and care plans from ambient listening during patient interactions. Early pilots at Cedars-Sinai show 40% reduction in nursing documentation time. (2) Patient monitoring: AI-enhanced clinical surveillance systems (discussed in DOC-011 for sepsis) alert nurses to early deterioration. The Modified Early Warning Score (MEWS), when enhanced with AI, reduces in-hospital cardiac arrests by 15-20% by detecting subtle trends in vital signs that fall between manual assessment intervals. (3) Care coordination: AI tools that predict patient discharge readiness, identify fall risks, and optimize medication administration timing help nurses prioritize care for their patient assignments. Challenges from the nursing perspective: (1) Alert fatigue — nurses receive an average of 150-300 clinical alerts per 12-hour shift, and AI adds to this burden unless carefully calibrated. Best practices suggest limiting AI alerts to high-confidence, high-severity events. (2) Workflow integration — AI tools that require separate logins, different screens, or interrupt established routines face poor adoption. (3) Job anxiety — 34% of nurses surveyed by the ANA expressed concern that AI might reduce nursing positions, though the current evidence suggests AI augments nursing capacity during a severe shortage rather than replacing roles. (4) Training — nursing education must evolve to include AI literacy, clinical informatics, and the ability to critically evaluate AI recommendations. The ANA's 2025 position statement on AI calls for nurses to be included in AI governance, vendor selection, and workflow design decisions.

documents.title: The Promise and Peril of Direct-to-Consumer Health AI Apps
documents.text: Direct-to-consumer (DTC) health AI apps have exploded, with over 350,000 health-related apps available on the Apple App Store and Google Play as of 2025. While most are simple wellness trackers, a growing category uses AI to provide clinical-grade health assessments without a healthcare provider. Examples include: SkinVision (skin cancer risk assessment from smartphone photos, 2.5 million users), Eko (AI stethoscope for heart murmur detection, consumer version launched 2025), and Evidation (continuous health assessment from smartphone and wearable data, predicting disease risk). The FDA has struggled to regulate this space. Its 2023 guidance on 'Clinical Decision Support Software' exempted tools that provide recommendations for users to discuss with their healthcare provider — a loophole that many DTC apps exploit. The risks are real: a 2025 Consumer Reports investigation found that 4 of 7 tested AI skin cancer apps missed melanomas in darker skin tones, and 2 of 5 AI symptom checkers failed to recommend emergency care for symptoms consistent with heart attack in women. Consumer protection advocates argue for mandatory clinical validation standards, while industry groups warn that over-regulation will stifle innovation. The middle ground emerging is a tiered regulatory framework: 'informational' apps (calorie counting, step tracking) remain unregulated, 'advisory' apps (AI symptom checkers, skin analysis) require clinical validation and transparent accuracy disclosures, and 'diagnostic' apps (those claiming to diagnose or rule out specific conditions) require FDA clearance. The UK's MHRA and EU's MDR have already adopted versions of this tiered approach. US legislation is expected in 2026.
